initial_program:
% -------------------------------------------------
%  Grid dimensions (the hidden puzzles are 57  33)
% -------------------------------------------------
n_rows(N)    :- N = #count{R : input(R,_,_) }.
n_cols(M)    :- M = #count{C : input(_,C,_)} .
row(I)       :- I = 0..N-1 .
col(J)       :- J = 0..M-1 .

% The output is a centred 33 subgrid.
% We keep the first three rows and the first three columns that
% contain at least one nonzero cell.
output_cell(R,C) :- row(R), col(C), R < 3, C < 3 .

% Each output cell gets exactly one colour
1 { output(R,C,Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Rules that encode the discovered pattern.
%
%   * Let C be the colour that appears in the input (the only
%     nonzero colour in all training examples).
%   * Let Rmid be the *middle* row that contains colour C
%     (i.e. the row for which the number of rows with colour C
%     on its left is equal to the number of rows with colour C on its
%     right).  The same for Cmid  the middle column that contains C.
%   * An output cell is coloured C  iff  it lies on the
%     middle row OR on the middle column.
%   * Every other output cell must be 0.
% -------------------------------------------------
% Find the (unique) colour that appears in the input (ignoring 0).
color_used(C) :- input(_,_,C), C != 0, 
                 not( C1 = 0 ), 
                 not( C2 != C, color_used(C2) ).

% Determine rows that contain colour C
row_has_C(R) :- row(R), 
                input(R,_,C), C != 0 .

% Determine columns that contain colour C
col_has_C(Ccol) :- col(Ccol),
                    input(_,Ccol,C), C != 0 .

% Find the middle row (the row whose index is the median of rows_with_C)
mid_row(Rmid) :-
    row_has_C(Rmid),
    #count{X : row_has_C(X), X < Rmid} = #count{Y : row_has_C(Y), Y > Rmid}.

% Find the middle column
mid_col(Cmid) :-
    col_has_C(Cmid),
    #count{X : col_has_C(X), X < Cmid} = #count{Y : col_has_C(Y), Y > Cmid}.

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Encode the plusshape
% -------------------------------------------------
% If the output cell lies on the middle row or the middle column,
% its colour must be C.
output(R,C,C) :-
    output_cell(R,C),
    (R = Rmid ; C = Cmid)  % the centre cell  always coloured
    ;
output(R,C,C) :-
    output_cell(R,C),
    (R = Rmid ; R != Rmid) ,   % any cell on the middle row
    C = color_used(C) .        % colour C is the one that appears in the input

output(R,C,C) :-
    output_cell(R,C),
    (C = Cmid ; C != Cmid) .   % any cell on the middle column
    C = color_used(C) .

% Any other cell must be colour 0.
output(R,C,0) :-
    output_cell(R,C),
    R != Rmid, C != Cmid .

% Ensure consistency of colour assignments (every cell gets exactly one colour)
:- not( output(R1,C1,V1), output(R2,C2,V2),
        R1 = R2, C1 = C2, V1 != V2 ).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Output the grid (only the output cells are visible)
% -------------------------------------------------
#show output/3.

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Grid dimensions (the hidden puzzles are 57  33)
% -------------------------------------------------
n_rows(N)    :- N = #count{R : input(R,_,_) }.
n_cols(M)    :- M = #count{C : input(_,C,_)} .
row(I)       :- I = 0..N-1 .
col(J)       :- J = 0..M-1 .

% The output is a centred 33 subgrid.
% We keep the first three rows and the first three columns that
% contain at least one nonzero cell.
output_cell(R,C) :- row(R), col(C), R < 3, C < 3 .

% Each output cell gets exactly one colour
1 { output(R,C,Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Rules that encode the discovered pattern.
%
%   * Let C be the colour that appears in the input (the only
%     nonzero colour in all training examples).
%   * Let Rmid be the *middle* row that contains colour C
%     (i.e. the row for which the number of rows with colour C
%     on its left is equal to the number of rows with colour C on its
%     right).  The same for Cmid  the middle column that contains C.
%   * An output cell is coloured C  iff  it lies on the
%     middle row OR on the middle column.
%   * Every other output cell must be 0.
% -------------------------------------------------
% Find the (unique) colour that appears in the input (ignoring 0).
color_used(C) :- input(_,_,C), C != 0, 
                 not( C1 = 0 ), 
                 not( C2 != C, color_used(C2) ).

% Determine rows that contain colour C
row_has_C(R) :- row(R), 
                input(R,_,C), C != 0 .

% Determine columns that contain colour C
col_has_C(Ccol) :- col(Ccol),
                    input(_,Ccol,C), C != 0 .

% Find the middle row (the row whose index is the median of rows_with_C)
mid_row(Rmid) :-
    row_has_C(Rmid),
    #count{X : row_has_C(X), X < Rmid} = #count{Y : row_has_C(Y), Y > Rmid}.

% Find the middle column
mid_col(Cmid) :-
    col_has_C(Cmid),
    #count{X : col_has_C(X), X < Cmid} = #count{Y : col_has_C(Y), Y > Cmid}.

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Encode the plusshape
% -------------------------------------------------
% If the output cell lies on the middle row or the middle column,
% its colour must be C.
output(R,C,C) :-
    output_cell(R,C),
    (R = Rmid ; C = Cmid)  % the centre cell  always coloured
    ;
output(R,C,C) :-
    output_cell(R,C),
    (R = Rmid ; R != Rmid) ,   % any cell on the middle row
    C = color_used(C) .        % colour C is the one that appears in the input

output(R,C,C) :-
    output_cell(R,C),
    (C = Cmid ; C != Cmid) .   % any cell on the middle column
    C = color_used(C) .

% Any other cell must be colour 0.
output(R,C,0) :-
    output_cell(R,C),
    R != Rmid, C != Cmid .

% Ensure consistency of colour assignments (every cell gets exactly one colour)
:- not( output(R1,C1,V1), output(R2,C2,V2),
        R1 = R2, C1 = C2, V1 != V2 ).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Output the grid (only the output cells are visible)
% -------------------------------------------------
#show output/3.

1: % -------------------------------------------------
2: %  Grid dimensions (the hidden puzzles are 57  33)
3: % -------------------------------------------------
4: n_rows(N)    :- N = #count{R : input(R,_,_) }.
5: n_cols(M)    :- M = #count{C : input(_,C,_)} .
6: row(I)       :- I = 0..N-1 .
7: col(J)       :- J = 0..M-1 .
8: 
9: % The output is a centred 33 subgrid.
10: % We keep the first three rows and the first three columns that
11: % contain at least one nonzero cell.
12: output_cell(R,C) :- row(R), col(C), R < 3, C < 3 .
13: 
14: % Each output cell gets exactly one colour
15: 1 { output(R,C,Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
16: 
17: % -------------------------------------------------
18: %  Rules that encode the discovered pattern.
19: %
20: %   * Let C be the colour that appears in the input (the only
21: %     nonzero colour in all training examples).
22: %   * Let Rmid be the *middle* row that contains colour C
23: %     (i.e. the row for which the number of rows with colour C
24: %     on its left is equal to the number of rows with colour C on its
25: %     right).  The same for Cmid  the middle column that contains C.
26: %   * An output cell is coloured C  iff  it lies on the
27: %     middle row OR on the middle column.
28: %   * Every other output cell must be 0.
29: % -------------------------------------------------
30: % Find the (unique) colour that appears in the input (ignoring 0).
31: color_used(C) :- input(_,_,C), C != 0.
32: 
33: % Determine rows that contain colour C
34: row_has_C(R) :- row(R), 
35:                 input(R,_,C), C != 0 .
36: 
37: % Determine columns that contain colour C
38: col_has_C(Ccol) :- col(Ccol),
39:                     input(_,Ccol,C), C != 0 .
40: 
41: % Find the middle row (the row whose index is the median of rows_with_C)
42: left_cnt(Rmid) :- #count{X : row_has_C(X), X < Rmid}.
43: right_cnt(Rmid) :- #count{Y : row_has_C(Y), Y > Rmid}.
44: mid_row(Rmid) :-
45:     row_has_C(Rmid),
46:     left_cnt(Rmid),
47:     right_cnt(Rmid),
48:     left_cnt(Rmid) = right_cnt(Rmid).
49: 
50: % Find the middle column
51: leftc_cnt(Cmid) :- #count{Z : col_has_C(Z), Z < Cmid}.
52: rightc_cnt(Cmid) :- #count{W : col_has_C(W), W > Cmid}.
53: mid_col(Cmid) :-
54:     col_has_C(Cmid),
55:     leftc_cnt(Cmid),
56:     rightc_cnt(Cmid),
57:     leftc_cnt(Cmid) = rightc_cnt(Cmid).
58: 
59: % -------------------------------------------------
60: %  Encode the plusshape
61: % -------------------------------------------------
62: % If the output cell lies on the middle row or the middle column,
63: % its colour must be C.
64: output(R,C,C) :-
65:     output_cell(R,C),
66:     (R = Rmid, C = Cmid),
67:     C = color_used(C).
68: 
69: % Any cell on the middle row (including centre) must have colour C
70: output(R,C,C) :-
71:     output_cell(R,C),
72:     R = Rmid,
73:     C != Cmid,
74:     C = color_used(C).
75: 
76: % Any cell on the middle column (including centre) must have colour C
77: output(R,C,C) :-
78:     output_cell(R,C),
79:     C = Cmid,
80:     R != Rmid,
81:     C = color_used(C).
82: 
83: % Any other cell must be colour 0.
84: output(R,C,0) :-
85:     output_cell(R,C),
86:     R != Rmid,
87:     C != Cmid.
88: 
89: % Ensure consistency of colour assignments (every cell gets exactly one colour)
90: :- not(output(R1,C1,V1), output(R2,C2,V2), R1 = R2, C1 = C2, V1 != V2).

92: % -------------------------------------------------
93: %  Output the grid (only the output cells are visible)

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Grid dimensions (the hidden puzzles are 57  33)
% -------------------------------------------------
n_rows(N)    :- N = #count{R : input(R,_,_) }.
n_cols(M)    :- M = #count{C : input(_,C,_)} .
row(I)       :- I = 0..N-1 .
col(J)       :- J = 0..M-1 .

% The output is a centred 33 subgrid.
% We keep the first three rows and the first three columns that
% contain at least one nonzero cell.
output_cell(R,C) :- row(R), col(C), R < 3, C < 3 .

% Each output cell gets exactly one colour
1 { output(R,C,Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Rules that encode the discovered pattern.
%
%   * Let C be the colour that appears in the input (the only
%     nonzero colour in all training examples).
%   * Let Rmid be the *middle* row that contains colour C
%     (i.e. the row for which the number of rows with colour C
%     on its left is equal to the number of rows with colour C on its
%     right).  The same for Cmid  the middle column that contains C.
%   * An output cell is coloured C  iff  it lies on the
%     middle row OR on the middle column.
%   * Every other output cell must be 0.
% -------------------------------------------------
% Find the (unique) colour that appears in the input (ignoring 0).
color_used(C) :- input(_,_,C), C != 0, 
                 not( C1 = 0 ), 
                 not( C2 != C, color_used(C2) ).

% Determine rows that contain colour C
row_has_C(R) :- row(R), 
                input(R,_,C), C != 0 .

% Determine columns that contain colour C
col_has_C(Ccol) :- col(Ccol),
                    input(_,Ccol,C), C != 0 .

% Find the middle row (the row whose index is the median of rows_with_C)
mid_row(Rmid) :-
    row_has_C(Rmid),
    #count{X : row_has_C(X), X < Rmid} = #count{Y : row_has_C(Y), Y > Rmid}.

% Find the middle column
mid_col(Cmid) :-
    col_has_C(Cmid),
    #count{X : col_has_C(X), X < Cmid} = #count{Y : col_has_C(Y), Y > Cmid}.

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Encode the plusshape
% -------------------------------------------------
% If the output cell lies on the middle row or the middle column,
% its colour must be C.
output(R,C,C) :-
    output_cell(R,C),
    (R = Rmid ; C = Cmid)  % the centre cell  always coloured
    ;
output(R,C,C) :-
    output_cell(R,C),
    (R = Rmid ; R != Rmid) ,   % any cell on the middle row
    C = color_used(C) .        % colour C is the one that appears in the input

output(R,C,C) :-
    output_cell(R,C),
    (C = Cmid ; C != Cmid) .   % any cell on the middle column
    C = color_used(C) .

% Any other cell must be colour 0.
output(R,C,0) :-
    output_cell(R,C),
    R != Rmid, C != Cmid .

% Ensure consistency of colour assignments (every cell gets exactly one colour)
:- not( output(R1,C1,V1), output(R2,C2,V2),
        R1 = R2, C1 = C2, V1 != V2 ).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Output the grid (only the output cells are visible)
% -------------------------------------------------
#show output/3.
